steps.title: Identify the pattern the researcher predicts
steps.content: The researcher proposes that as humans migrated from Africa outward, each new language retained fewer sounds from the original. This predicts a distance-based pattern: languages closer to Africa should have more sounds; languages farther away should have fewer.

steps.title: Find what breaks this pattern
steps.content:
Choice B — Languages that emerged in Europe and Western Asia tend to have more sounds than languages that emerged in Africa do. directly contradicts the prediction by showing that Europe and Western Asia—farther from Africa—have more sounds than African languages, when the researcher's logic predicts they should have fewer.

Choices A, C, and D either support the pattern, address consistency within regions rather than the distance relationship, or describe variation between regions without testing whether distance determines sound count.

That matches Choice B — Languages that emerged in Europe and Western Asia tend to have more sounds than languages that emerged in Africa do..
